lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.description: Say hello and goodbye in Spanish

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Hello' in Japanese (daytime)?
questions.explanation: こんにちは (Konnichiwa) is used from midday to early evening. It literally means 'As for today...'
おはよう (Ohayou) means Good morning. For more formal situations, say おはようございます (Ohayou gozaimasu).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Thank you' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: ありがとう (Arigatou) means thank you. The polite form is ありがとうございます (Arigatou gozaimasu).
さようなら (Sayounara) means goodbye. For casual farewells, Japanese people often say またね (Matane) — see you later!

questions.prompt: Complete: '___ は Kobanwa — meaning Good Evening in Japanese
questions.explanation: こんばんは (Konbanwa) is the evening greeting, used from dusk onwards.
いち (ichi) = 1. The sequence starts: ichi, ni, san, shi/yon...
ご (go) = 5. Remember the counting: ichi(1), ni(2), san(3), shi(4), go(5)!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'ten' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: じゅう (juu) = 10. Japanese counting: nana(7), hachi(8), ku(9), juu(10).

questions.prompt: いち, に, さん, ___, ご (Fill in four)
questions.explanation: し (shi) or よん (yon) = 4. Both are correct! よん is more common in everyday speech.

questions.prompt: Which hiragana character reads as 'a' (like in 'car')?
questions.explanation: あ (a) is the first hiragana vowel. The 5 vowels are: あ(a), い(i), う(u), え(e), お(o).
か = 'ka'. The ka-row: か(ka), き(ki), く(ku), け(ke), こ(ko).
す (su) is in the sa-row: さ(sa), し(shi), す(su), せ(se), そ(so).

questions.prompt: What does the hiragana 'な (na)' sound like?
questions.explanation: な (na) is in the na-row. Together: な(na), に(ni), ぬ(nu), ね(ne), の(no).
みず (mizu) = water. お水 (omizu) is the polite form you'd use in a restaurant.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'delicious' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: おいしい (oishii) = delicious! Every chef in Japan loves to hear this.
ラーメン (raamen) is Japanese ramen — noodle soup that is beloved worldwide!

questions.prompt: すし is the Japanese word for ___
questions.explanation: すし (sushi) — one of Japan's most famous dishes, made with vinegared rice and toppings.
あか (aka) = red. Japan's iconic red torii gates are called 赤い鳥居 (akai torii)!
あお (ao) means blue (and sometimes green — Japanese has an interesting color history!)

questions.prompt: How do you say 'white' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: しろ (shiro) = white. Mount Fuji's snow cap is called 白い雪 (shiroi yuki) — white snow!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Excuse me / Sorry' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: すみません (sumimasen) works for both 'excuse me' and 'I'm sorry' — super useful!
どうぞ (douzo) = please go ahead / here you are. Used when offering something or letting someone pass.

questions.prompt: What is 'I don't understand' in Japanese?
questions.explanation: わかりません (wakarimasen) = I don't understand. Very useful when learning! わかります = I understand.

questions.prompt: Which is the most formal greeting in English?
questions.explanation: 'Good morning' is polite and professional. Use it in offices, meetings, and with elders.

questions.prompt: How do you introduce yourself formally?
questions.explanation: 'My name is [Name]' is the standard formal introduction. 'I'm [Name]' is the informal version.
'Nice to meet you!' is said when meeting someone for the first time. A universally friendly phrase!
'I'm fine, thank you' or 'I'm doing well, thanks!' are the classic polite responses.

questions.prompt: How do you say 12:30 in English?
questions.explanation: 'Half past twelve' = 12:30. 'Quarter past' = :15. 'Quarter to' = :45.
Nineteen (19) → Twenty (20). The -teen numbers: thirteen, fourteen... nineteen. Then twenty!

questions.prompt: One, two, three, ___, five (fill in four)
questions.explanation: Four (4) — spelled F-O-U-R. Common mistake: don't confuse with 'for' or 'fore'!
A noun is a person, place, or thing. 'Table' is a thing. 'Run' is a verb, 'beautiful' is adjective, 'quickly' is adverb.
'Children' is an irregular plural. English has many: man→men, woman→women, tooth→teeth, foot→feet.
Library is a place. 'Friendship' and 'happiness' are abstract nouns. 'Running' is a verb/gerund.

questions.prompt: Which is correct: 'She ___ to school every day.'
questions.explanation: With he/she/it, add -s or -es to the verb: 'She goes', 'He plays', 'It works'. This is the third-person singular rule!
With 'I', the verb stays in base form: 'I study', 'I work', 'I learn'.
Present simple = habits, facts, routines. 'The sun rises in the east.' 'She drinks coffee every morning.'

questions.prompt: Which question word asks about a person?
questions.explanation: 'Who' asks about people. 'What' = things. 'Where' = places. 'When' = time. 'Why' = reason. 'How' = manner.

questions.prompt: '___ is your birthday?' — which word fits?
questions.explanation: 'When is your birthday?' — 'When' asks about time. 'What is your birthday?' would be grammatically odd.

questions.prompt: '___ do you come from?' (Asking about place of origin)
questions.explanation: 'Where do you come from?' asks about place. Could also say 'Where are you from?' — both are correct!

questions.prompt: How do you politely order at a restaurant?
questions.explanation: 'I'll have..., please' is polite and natural. 'Could I have...' or 'I'd like...' also work perfectly.

options: The check / invoice for your meal
questions.correctAnswer: The check / invoice for your meal
questions.explanation: 'The bill' (UK) or 'The check' (US) = the invoice at a restaurant. Both are widely understood.

questions.prompt: What is the standard French greeting?
questions.explanation: Bonjour = Hello (daytime). Bonsoir = Good evening. Au revoir = Goodbye. Merci = Thank you.
'Comment allez-vous?' is the formal 'How are you?'. The informal version is 'Ça va?' (Sah vah?)

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Thank you very much' in French?
questions.explanation: Merci beaucoup = Thank you very much. De rien = You're welcome. S'il vous plaît = Please.
Au revoir = Goodbye. Literally 'until we see each other again'. For casual farewell: Salut! (Hi/Bye)
cinq (sank) = 5. French numbers 1-5: un, deux, trois, quatre, cinq.
dix (deese) = 10. The French 6-10: six, sept, huit, neuf, dix.

questions.prompt: un, deux, trois, ___, cinq (Fill in four)
questions.explanation: quatre (kah-truh) = 4. Notice the silent 're' at the end — typical French pronunciation!
rouge (roozh) = red. You may know it from lipstick brands or 'Moulin Rouge' in Paris!
bleu (bluh) = blue. The French flag (tricolore) has bleu, blanc, rouge — blue, white, red!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'white' in French?
questions.explanation: blanc (blon) = white (masculine). blanche = white (feminine). French adjectives change gender!

questions.prompt: How do you order a coffee in French?
questions.explanation: 'Un café, s'il vous plaît' = A coffee, please. Always add 's'il vous plaît' for politeness!
pain (pan) = bread. The French baguette is a type of pain. Pain au chocolat = chocolate bread pastry!

questions.prompt: What does 'L'addition, s'il vous plaît' mean?
questions.explanation: L'addition = the bill/check. Just like saying 'check please' in English — use it at the end of your meal.
mère (mair) = mother. père = father. frère = brother. sœur = sister.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my brother' in French?
questions.explanation: mon frère = my brother. 'Mon' is masculine possessive, 'ma' is feminine. frère (freir) = brother.
Je suis = I am. From the verb 'être' (to be): je suis, tu es, il/elle est, nous sommes, vous êtes, ils sont.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'I have' in French?
questions.explanation: J'ai = I have. From 'avoir' (to have): j'ai, tu as, il a, nous avons, vous avez, ils ont.

questions.prompt: Je ___ étudiant. (I am a student — fill in être form)
questions.explanation: Je suis étudiant = I am a student. Note: no article needed in French! (Not 'un étudiant' after être.)

options: I bow to the divine in you
questions.correctAnswer: I bow to the divine in you
questions.explanation: Namaste (नमस्ते) comes from 'namas' (bow/salutation) + 'te' (to you). A profound greeting recognizing the divine in everyone.

questions.prompt: What is 'How are you?' in Sanskrit?
questions.explanation: कुशलं ते? (Kushalam te?) = How are you? / Are you well? 'Kushalam' means 'well/auspicious'.
धन्यवाद (Dhanyavaad) = Thank you. It comes from 'dhanya' (blessed/grateful) + 'vaad' (word/speech).
स्वागतम् (Svagatam) = Welcome! You'll see this at the entrance of temples and heritage sites across India.

questions.prompt: Sanskrit has how many basic vowels (svaras)?
questions.explanation: Sanskrit has 16 vowels (svaras): अ, आ, इ, ई, उ, ऊ, ऋ, ॠ, ऌ, ॡ, ए, ऐ, ओ, औ, अं, अः — each with precise phonetic rules.
अ is a short 'a' sound, similar to the 'a' in 'father' or 'cut'. It's the most fundamental Sanskrit vowel.

questions.prompt: What is the long form of 'इ (i)'?
questions.explanation: इ = short 'i' (as in 'it'). ई = long 'ee' (as in 'feet'). Sanskrit distinguishes short and long vowels precisely.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'one' in Sanskrit?
questions.explanation: एक (eka) = 1. You'll recognize it in words like 'ekam' (unity) and 'Ekta' (name meaning unity).
त्रि (tri) = 3. This root appears in English too: tri-angle, tri-cycle, tri-dent. Languages are connected!
दश (dasha) = 10. From this root comes the English prefix 'deca-' (decade, decagon)! Sanskrit is a mother language.
जल (jala) = water. Also called 'ap' or 'ambu'. Water is one of the Panchamahabhuta — the five great elements.
अग्नि (agni) = fire. Agni is also the Hindu god of fire. This word root appears in 'ignite' in English!
आकाश (akaasha) = sky/ether/space. It's the 5th element (Panchamahabhuta). 'Aakash' is a common name meaning 'sky'.
Panchamahabhuta = The 5 Great Elements: Earth (पृथ्वी), Water (जल), Fire (अग्नि), Air (वायु), Space (आकाश).
हस्त (hasta) = hand. You'll see this in yoga poses: 'hasta mudra' (hand gesture). Also root of 'hasta la vista'!
नेत्र (netra) = eye. The third eye concept in Hinduism is called 'तृतीय नेत्र' (tritiya netra). नेत्र is also a common name.
हृदय (hridaya) = heart. This is the root of 'hrid' in many Indian languages. It means both the physical heart and the spiritual core.
Sanskrit uses SOV order: 'रामः फलं खादति' = Rama fruit eats (Rama eats fruit). The verb comes at the END!

questions.prompt: What is 'Rama goes' in Sanskrit structure?
questions.explanation: Sanskrit is highly flexible! Due to its case system (vibhakti), word order can vary without changing meaning. रामः गच्छति = Standard SOV. गच्छति रामः = also valid!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'Hello' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Hola' is the most common Spanish greeting, used at any time of day!
'Buenas tardes' is used from noon until around 9 PM in Spanish.
'Buenas noches' = Good night. Buenas (good, feminine plural) + noches (nights).
'Hasta luego' literally means 'until later' — a common casual farewell.

questions.prompt: How do you ask 'How are you?' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: '¿Cómo estás?' (informal) or '¿Cómo está usted?' (formal) both ask 'How are you?'
cinco = 5. Remember: uno, dos, tres, cuatro, cinco!
'Diez' is ten — the first milestone in Spanish counting!
cuatro = four. Sequence: uno(1), dos(2), tres(3), cuatro(4), cinco(5).
'Rojo' means red in Spanish. Remember: traffic lights are rojo!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'blue' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Azul' means blue. Think of the azure sky!
amarillo = yellow. The Spanish sunflower (girasol) is amarillo!
madre = mother. padre = father. hermano = brother. hermana = sister.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'my brother' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: mi hermano = my brother. 'Mi' = my (same for masculine and feminine). hermano = brother.

questions.prompt: How do you say 'please' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: por favor = please. gracias = thank you. de nada = you're welcome. lo siento = I'm sorry.
Lo siento = I'm sorry / I feel it. 'Sentir' means to feel — expressing that you feel someone's pain.
agua = water. Una botella de agua = a bottle of water. Essential phrase for any Spanish-speaking country!

questions.prompt: How do you say 'the bill, please' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: La cuenta, por favor = The bill, please. Essential phrase for restaurants in Spanish-speaking countries!
